summary: An approved permit would represent a second, and potentially final, bite at the apple for EQT and its six-well Heracles operation. The project has been at the center of a three-year legal battle that pitted the company and township commissioners against local environmental advocates. The post EQT wants Elizabeth Township’s approval for fracked wells near a school. Residents are worried. appeared first on Pittsburgh's Public Source . headline: In Larimer, developers turn their focus to community spaces
summary: After years of planning and restoration, Food21 will start construction on transforming the now-defunct Larimer School auditorium and gym into Celebration Hall. The project aims to return the space to community use, with event space, a food hall and a demonstration kitchen supported by local culinary entrepreneurs. The post In Larimer, developers turn their focus to community spaces appeared first on Pittsburgh's Public Source . PublicSource is a nonprofit news organization serving the Pitt

headline: O'Connor says Pittsburgh budget short some $30 million in key areas
summary: Pittsburgh Mayor Corey O'Connor announced that the city's 2026 budget requires an immediate overhaul after his team identified $30–$40 million in underestimated costs since taking office in January, including shortfalls in employee health care, retiree health care, bridge upkeep, fuel, and fleet repair. He pledged to revise the budget starting in March by examining unfilled positions and trimming spending, while ruling out further tax hikes, layoffs, or cuts to core services like violence prevention or affordable housing. The announcement followed an unaudited report showing the city ended 2025 with an $8.6 million deficit and spent more than $20 million over budget on overtime.

headline: Pittsburgh will reopen its 2026 budget to 'address serious concerns'
summary: Mayor Corey O'Connor announced Thursday that he will reopen the city's already-adopted 2026 budget to address serious concerns, a rare move typically reserved for contract adjustments. The decision follows Controller Rachel Heistler's warnings that Pittsburgh is overspending and under-collecting revenue, and comes after council raised property taxes 20% in late 2025. O'Connor said the city is projecting a $30–$40 million gap and will examine every contract and unfilled position to close the deficit without new taxes or layoffs.

headline: Post-Penguins planning for Pittsburgh's Lower Hill begins
summary: The Urban Redevelopment Authority of Pittsburgh formally took over Lower Hill development rights from the Pittsburgh Penguins and voted to seek proposals for approximately seven acres between Bedford and Centre Avenues to build affordable and market-rate housing on what is currently surface parking. The URA will also formalize site control of 1.75 acres at Centre Avenue and Crawford Street to advance the Bedford Dwellings redevelopment, a mixed-income housing project funded by a federal Choice Neighborhoods grant. Nearly $1 million in reinvestment funds was approved for four Hill District projects including African Queens Apartments, Big Tom's Barbershop rehabilitation at 2178 Centre, the Rhythm Square artist studios at 2239 Centre, and a mixed-use building at 2932 Wylie Ave.

headline: Nobody's building offices in Pittsburgh. Housing may benefit.
summary: Pittsburgh's commercial development has slowed sharply since the pandemic, with approximately 17% of citywide office space vacant, prompting developers like Oxford Development to shift planned office buildings in the Strip District to residential use. Downtown Pittsburgh is exploring publicly funded office-to-residential conversions to add foot traffic and affordability, with conversions planned at locations including 901-903 Liberty Ave. and 120 Cecil Way. Regional data shows nearly 6,000 residential building permits were issued last year, up from pre-pandemic levels, suggesting housing demand is outpacing office development.

headline: State officials pledge nearly $3.5M to Pittsburgh Downtown Partnership to support new businesses
summary: The Pennsylvania Department of Community and Economic Development is directing nearly $3.5 million in tax credits over six years to the Pittsburgh Downtown Partnership through the Neighborhood Assistance Program. The funding will support street cleaning services, business recruitment, and the creation of a new dining district at Market Square, and will expand a rent abatement program aimed at bringing 30 new businesses downtown. Partnership leaders framed the investment as critical to reversing downtown's post-pandemic struggles.

headline: PRT to Hold Public Meeting on Belasco Station Project
summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit announced an in-person public meeting for March 26 at St. Catherine of Siena Church at 1810 Belasco Avenue regarding planned upgrades to the Belasco Light Rail Station on the Red Line in the Beechview neighborhood. The meeting will cover existing conditions and updates to improvement plans first presented in 2020; the station sits on Broadway Avenue where light rail vehicles run parallel to vehicle traffic. The station is not currently ADA accessible, and PRT will provide ASL interpretation at the meeting.

headline: Shaler Area preparing to borrow $7.5M to pay for capital projects including roof at middle school
summary: The Shaler Area School District is preparing to borrow $7.5 million to fund a range of capital projects, including roof replacement at the middle school and safety and auditorium upgrades at Shaler Area High School projected at approximately $100,000. The district has a $1.3 million state reimbursement grant supporting these infrastructure repairs. The borrowing reflects broader pressures on suburban school districts across Allegheny County to maintain aging facilities while managing tight budgets.

headline: "Growth as a priority:" Two O'Connor officials share their business development plans for Pittsburgh
summary: Pittsburgh economic development officials Steve Wray and Mary McKinney Flaherty outlined the O'Connor administration's strategies for business recruitment, permitting reform, and economic growth at a NAIOP Pittsburgh panel discussion. The officials described a pro-growth philosophy centered on streamlining development approvals and attracting new businesses and residents, characterizing growth as the administration's top priority. The presentation reflected O'Connor's broader campaign to reposition Pittsburgh as open for business after years of developer frustration under the prior Gainey administration.

headline: Sara Innamorato: Safe, affordable housing for every county resident
summary: Allegheny County Executive Sara Innamorato outlined her HOUSING for All framework, describing rising costs, stagnant wages, aging housing stock, absentee landlords, and private equity as drivers of a housing crisis requiring a coordinated countywide response. She highlighted the county's Housing Investment Fund—designed to raise $50–$100 million—and the 500 in 500 Initiative that has housed more than 620 people exiting shelter. Innamorato called for preservation of existing affordable housing through home repair and lead remediation, alongside anti-displacement strategies to protect vulnerable communities.

headline: After Penguins exit, Pittsburgh moves to revive Lower Hill with housing proposals
summary: After decades of stagnation on the 28-acre former Civic Arena site, the URA opened a public comment period on plans that include a 140-unit apartment complex with half the units designated affordable and a broader request for proposals covering another seven acres. The push follows the expiration of the Pittsburgh Penguins' development rights in October 2025, and officials also announced that all future property tax revenue from the site will be reinvested locally. Some Hill District residents remain cautious after decades of past delays and displacement.

headline: New development envisioned for Westinghouse site in Churchill
summary: Commonwealth Commercial Real Estate presented early renderings of Legacy Crossings, a proposed walkable mixed-use development on the former Westinghouse Research and Technology Park in Churchill Borough, to the Churchill Borough Council. Plans include wholesale and retail grocery stores, restaurants, a gas station, parkland, a town center, medical offices, and residential units, with an emphasis on non-auto-centric design. Churchill Borough is also considering zoning amendments to block data centers, vape shops, and tattoo parlors following past community opposition to an Amazon distribution center on the site.

headline: URA to Advance Lower Hill Redevelopment Process; Board Set to Vote on $930,000 to Support Four Transformative Development Projects in the Greater Hill District
summary: The URA formally announced it will issue a Request for Proposals for the redevelopment of 6.82 acres on the Lower Hill site (Blocks A and C) as surface parking is converted into affordable and/or market-rate housing with potential mixed-use and commercial components. The board also set a vote to approve $930,000 in grants from the Greater Hill District Neighborhood Reinvestment Fund to four community projects: African Queens Apartments ($250K), Big Tom's Barbershop rehabilitation ($240K), Rhythm Square artist studios ($250K), and a Communion LLC mixed-use building at 2932 Wylie Ave ($190K). Public comment on the draft RFP will be open from March 12 through April 7, 2026.

headline: More than 50 Pittsburgh Regional Transit bus routes to be detoured for St. Patrick's Day 2026
summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit announced that more than 50 bus routes will be detoured on Saturday, March 14, for the city's annual St. Patrick's Day Parade and related events, with most detours running from 7 a.m. to 2 p.m. and some lasting until 5 p.m. when police are expected to reopen streets. Because several bus routes will be unable to reach parts of Downtown, riders will be able to disembark and transfer for free to light-rail cars at Station Square or Penn Station, the bus and rail hub along the Martin Luther King Jr. East Busway. PRT will operate a free rail shuttle between Penn Station and Steel Plaza to accommodate riders during the event.

headline: Hazelwood Green: Attracting tech jobs, businesses, economic activity — and homes for new residents
summary: Hazelwood Green, redeveloped by RIDC from the former LTV Steel mill site, is becoming a nationally recognized hub for robotics, artificial intelligence, and life sciences with the official opening of Carnegie Mellon University's $100 million, 150,000-square-foot Robotics Innovation Center adjacent to Mill 19. The University of Pittsburgh's BioForge building, spanning 185,000 square feet, is nearing completion and will be anchored by ElevateBio to advance biomedical manufacturing. Residential projects and a Pittsburgh Steelers-backed community sports facility are also under construction on the site.

headline: Rep. Summer Lee Presents $3M Check to Support Affordable Housing in Allegheny County
summary: Congresswoman Summer L. Lee joined Allegheny County Executive Sara Innamorato and Lieutenant Governor Austin Davis at the Avenue Apartments in Braddock to present a $3 million Community Project Funding investment for affordable housing initiatives across Allegheny County. The funding aims to expand access to safe and affordable housing for working families, seniors, and children in the face of rising rents and homelessness. Officials emphasized the need for coordinated federal, state, and local efforts, pointing to the county's HOUSING for All executive order signed by Innamorato in February 2026.

headline: Pittsburgh Police, Fire, and EMS Respond to Shooting in Larimer
summary: Pittsburgh Police Zone 5, Pittsburgh Fire, and Pittsburgh EMS responded to the unit block of Auburn Street in the Larimer neighborhood after two ShotSpotter alerts detected thirteen rounds fired. Officers located an adult female inside a residence with a gunshot wound to her left thigh; she was transported to a local hospital in stable condition, and bullet damage was also found at the residence. The Mobile Crime Unit responded to process evidence, and Violent Crime Unit detectives are leading the ongoing investigation.

headline: O'Connor unveils overhaul of Pittsburgh's permitting process
summary: Mayor Corey O'Connor unveiled a three-phase overhaul of Pittsburgh's permitting and zoning systems, fulfilling a key campaign promise to make the city more development-friendly. The plan includes immediately expediting building permits, then revising the zoning code to remove obsolete sections, simplify height requirements, reduce zoning districts, and increase allowed heights in Urban Neighborhood Commercial districts. A third phase will modernize the certification process through online portals, with O'Connor noting that New York City issues permits in four to five weeks while Pittsburgh cannot.

headline: Pittsburgh launches zoning and permit overhaul
summary: Mayor Corey O'Connor announced a three-phase plan to ease building rules, spur housing growth, and streamline permit approvals, fulfilling a major campaign commitment to shift the city from a 'culture of no' to one of growth. Phase one immediately expedites building permits; phase two revises the zoning code to increase heights in Urban Neighborhood Commercial districts including the Baum Corridor in the East End; phase three consolidates applications through searchable online portals. O'Connor specified no citywide inclusionary zoning is planned, but targeted IZ near transit lines or in density-bonus areas remains possible.

headline: Pittsburgh colleges expand their footprint to meet demand
summary: Pittsburgh's major universities—Point Park, Duquesne, Pitt, and Carnegie Mellon—are acquiring and repurposing buildings across several neighborhoods to meet growing enrollment demand, including Point Park's plans for the former YWCA on Wood Street in Downtown and Pitt's BioForge facility at Hazelwood Green. Despite most university properties being tax-exempt, officials argue the expansions generate economic benefits through construction jobs, neighborhood vibrancy, and some taxable development. Officials from the universities note expansion projects are creating new space for employees, supporting private investment, and contributing to neighborhood safety.

headline: University Line Construction to Impact Traffic Patterns Along Fifth and Forbes Avenues Through the End of April
summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit announced that Phase Two University Line construction activity along Fifth Avenue between Bellefield and Robinson Streets and along Forbes Avenue between Jumonville and Birmingham Bridge will impact traffic patterns in Oakland and Uptown through the end of April. Beginning March 13, crews will mill, patch, and pave roadways and install new sidewalks, curbs, and ADA-compliant ramps; the bus stop at Fifth Avenue and Brenham Street will be taken out of service. Future phases will add PRTX stations, dedicated red bus lanes, upgraded traffic signals, and pedestrian safety improvements along the $291 million bus rapid transit corridor connecting Downtown, Uptown, and Oakland.

headline: Pittsburgh man charged in shooting death of sister in Greenfield neighborhood
summary: Aaron Charles Martin, 27, was charged with criminal homicide and related offenses following the fatal shooting of his sister, 26-year-old Charlee Martin, at an apartment complex on the 700 block of Montclair Street in Pittsburgh's Greenfield neighborhood in February 2026. Police found Charlee Martin dead inside the apartment after responding to a domestic dispute call, with multiple bullet casings around her body; Aaron Martin was found in a bedroom and taken into custody following a brief struggle. Additional charges including firearms violations and abuse of a corpse were filed after he was cleared from a hospital.

headline: Rising cost to build: Pittsburgh developers fighting challenges to build more multifamily in a tricky business climate
summary: Pittsburgh multifamily developers are navigating mounting challenges from higher labor, financing, and construction costs that are outpacing rent growth across the region, forcing builders including Oxford Development to pursue smaller, more financially viable projects. The cost pressures are constraining the housing supply pipeline at a time when demand remains strong and the city is pushing for more residential construction. Developers say creative financing strategies and public-private partnerships are increasingly necessary to make the numbers work on new projects.

headline: PRT's second draft of bus route changes increases frequency on core routes
summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit unveiled the Bus Line Refresh proposal, an updated redesign of the county's bus network shaped by feedback from more than 16,000 riders, operators, and community members that maintains familiar routes while improving frequency and reliability. Key improvements include 20% more Saturday service and 18% more Sunday service, 43 routes operating every 30 minutes or better (up from 27), and new one-seat rides to Oakland from communities including McCandless, Ross, Dormont, Brentwood, Millvale, Mount Lebanon, Carrick, and the West Busway. New routes include O35 Mt. Lebanon–Dormont, O45 Carrick–East Liberty, and O9 North Hills–Oakland; if approved by the PRT board this fall, implementation would begin in 2027.

headline: Bill to bar Allegheny County cooperation with ICE advances to council vote
summary: Allegheny County Council's public safety committee voted 4-3 to advance a bill that would prohibit county employees from cooperating with U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, bar ICE from housing detainees in the county jail without a judicial warrant, and restrict federal immigration officers from accessing county databases. The bill, sponsored by Councilwoman Bethany Hallam and colleagues, sparked about an hour of debate over existing policies, potential loss of federal funding under the Trump administration, and enforceability. Approximately 50 residents attended the meeting, applauding the committee's decision to advance it to a full council vote.

headline: PRT Bus Line Refresh: Familiar Routes, Stronger Connections — Updated Bus Network Proposal Launched
summary: Pittsburgh Regional Transit launched the Bus Line Refresh proposal, an evolution of the 2023 Bus Line Redesign effort that prioritizes increasing frequency and reliability on existing routes rather than a wholesale overhaul, following feedback from more than 16,000 riders and community members. The proposal reinvests more than 10% of service hours from low-ridership segments into higher-demand areas, with 195,000 residents in equity communities gaining 30-minute service—a 28% increase—and 380,000 additional jobs accessible at 30-minute frequency. PRT CEO Katharine Kelleman said more than 60 public meetings and pop-ups will be held across Allegheny County before required public hearings; if board-approved in fall 2026, implementation would begin in 2027.
